Math416Complex Variables (Lecture 3)

Math416 Lecture 3

Differentiation of functions in complex variables

Differentiability

Definition 2.1 of differentiability in complex variables

Suppose GGG is an open subset of C\mathbb{C}C. (very important, f′(z0)f'(z_0)f′(z0​) cannot be define unless z0z_0z0​ belongs to an open set in which fff is defined.)

A function f:G→Cf:G\to \mathbb{C}f:G→C is differentiable at z0∈Gz_0\in Gz0​∈G if

f′(z0)=lim⁡z→z0f(z)−f(z0)z−z0f'(z_0)=\lim_{z\to z_0}\frac{f(z)-f(z_0)}{z-z_0}f′(z0​)=z→z0​lim​z−z0​f(z)−f(z0​)​

exists.

Or equivalently,

We can also express the fff as f=u+ivf=u+ivf=u+iv, where u,v:G→Ru,v:G\to \mathbb{R}u,v:G→R are real-valued functions.

Recall that u:G→Ru:G\to \mathbb{R}u:G→R is differentiable at z0∈Gz_0\in Gz0​∈G if and only if there exists a complex number (x,y)∈C(x,y)\in \mathbb{C}(x,y)∈C such that a function

R(x,y)=u(x,y)−(u(x0,y0)+∂u∂x(x0,y0)(x−x0)+∂u∂y(x0,y0)(y−y0))R(x,y)=u(x,y)-\left(u(x_0,y_0)+\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)(x-x_0)+\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)(y-y_0)\right)R(x,y)=u(x,y)−(u(x0​,y0​)+∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)(x−x0​)+∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​)(y−y0​))

satisfies

lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣R(x,y)∣∣(x,y)−(x0,y0)∣=lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣R(x,y)∣(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2=0.\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|R(x,y)|}{|(x,y)-(x_0,y_0)|}=\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|R(x,y)|}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}=0.(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)lim​∣(x,y)−(x0​,y0​)∣∣R(x,y)∣​=(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)lim​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​∣R(x,y)∣​=0.

R(x,y)R(x,y)R(x,y) is the immediate result of mean value theorem applied to uuu at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​).

Theorem from 4111?

If uuu is differentiable at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​), then ∂u∂x(x0,y0)\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​) and ∂u∂y(x0,y0)\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​) exist.

If ∂u∂x(x0,y0)\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​) and ∂u∂y(x0,y0)\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​) exist and one of them is continuous at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​), then uuu is differentiable at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​).

lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣R(x,y)∣∣(x,y)−(x0,y0)∣=lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣u(x,y)−u(x0,y0)−∂u∂x(x0,y0)(x−x0)−∂u∂y(x0,y0)(y−y0)∣(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2=lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣u(x,y)−u(x0,y0)−∂u∂x(x0,y0)(x−x0)−∂u∂y(x0,y0)(y−y0)∣(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2\begin{aligned} \lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|R(x,y)|}{|(x,y)-(x_0,y_0)|}&=\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|u(x,y)-u(x_0,y_0)-\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)(x-x_0)-\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)(y-y_0)|}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}\\ &=\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|u(x,y)-u(x_0,y_0)-\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)(x-x_0)-\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)(y-y_0)|}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}\\ \end{aligned}(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)lim​∣(x,y)−(x0​,y0​)∣∣R(x,y)∣​​=(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)lim​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​∣u(x,y)−u(x0​,y0​)−∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)(x−x0​)−∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​)(y−y0​)∣​=(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)lim​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​∣u(x,y)−u(x0​,y0​)−∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)(x−x0​)−∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​)(y−y0​)∣​​

Let a(x,y)=∂u∂x(x,y)a(x,y)=\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x,y)a(x,y)=∂x∂u​(x,y) and b(x,y)=∂u∂y(x,y)b(x,y)=\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x,y)b(x,y)=∂y∂u​(x,y).

We can write R(x,y)R(x,y)R(x,y) as

R(x,y)=u(x,y)−u(x0,y0)−a(x,y)(x−x0)−b(x,y)(y−y0).R(x,y)=u(x,y)-u(x_0,y_0)-a(x,y)(x-x_0)-b(x,y)(y-y_0).R(x,y)=u(x,y)−u(x0​,y0​)−a(x,y)(x−x0​)−b(x,y)(y−y0​).

So lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣R(x,y)∣(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2=0\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|R(x,y)|}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}=0lim(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​∣R(x,y)∣​=0 if and only if lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)a(x−x0)(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2=0\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{a(x-x_0)}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}=0lim(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​a(x−x0​)​=0 and lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)b(y−y0)(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2=0\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{b(y-y_0)}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}=0lim(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​b(y−y0​)​=0.

On the imaginary part, we proceed similarly. Define

S(x,y)=v(x,y)−v(x0,y0)−∂v∂x(x0,y0)(x−x0)−∂v∂y(x0,y0)(y−y0).S(x,y)=v(x,y)-v(x_0,y_0)-\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)(x-x_0)-\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)(y-y_0).S(x,y)=v(x,y)−v(x0​,y0​)−∂x∂v​(x0​,y0​)(x−x0​)−∂y∂v​(x0​,y0​)(y−y0​).

Then the differentiability of vvv at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​) guarantees that

lim⁡(x,y)→(x0,y0)∣S(x,y)∣(x−x0)2+(y−y0)2=0.\lim_{(x,y)\to (x_0,y_0)}\frac{|S(x,y)|}{\sqrt{(x-x_0)^2+(y-y_0)^2}}=0.(x,y)→(x0​,y0​)lim​(x−x0​)2+(y−y0​)2​∣S(x,y)∣​=0.

Moreover, considering the definition of the complex derivative of f=u+ivf=u+ivf=u+iv, if we approach z0=x0+iy0z_0=x_0+iy_0z0​=x0​+iy0​ along different directions we obtain

f′(z0)=∂u∂x(x0,y0)+i∂v∂x(x0,y0)=∂v∂y(x0,y0)−i∂u∂y(x0,y0).f'(z_0)=\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)+i\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0) =\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)-i\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0).f′(z0​)=∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)+i∂x∂v​(x0​,y0​)=∂y∂v​(x0​,y0​)−i∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​).

Equating the real and imaginary parts of these two expressions forces

∂u∂x(x0,y0)=∂v∂y(x0,y0),∂u∂y(x0,y0)=−∂v∂x(x0,y0).\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)=\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0),\quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)=-\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0).∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)=∂y∂v​(x0​,y0​),∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​)=−∂x∂v​(x0​,y0​).

Theorem 2.6 (The Cauchy-Riemann equations):

If f=u+ivf=u+ivf=u+iv is complex differentiable at z0∈Gz_0\in Gz0​∈G, then uuu and vvv are real differentiable at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​) and

∂u∂x(x0,y0)=∂v∂y(x0,y0),∂u∂y(x0,y0)=−∂v∂x(x0,y0).\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)=\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0),\quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)=-\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0).∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)=∂y∂v​(x0​,y0​),∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​)=−∂x∂v​(x0​,y0​).

Some missing details:

The Cauchy-Riemann equations are necessary and sufficient for the differentiability of fff at z0z_0z0​.

This states that a function fff is complex differentiable at z0z_0z0​ if and only if uuu and vvv are real differentiable at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​) and the Cauchy-Riemann equations hold at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​). That is f′(z0)=∂u∂x(x0,y0)+i∂v∂x(x0,y0)=∂v∂y(x0,y0)−i∂u∂y(x0,y0)f'(z_0)=\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)+i\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)=\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)-i\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(x_0,y_0)f′(z0​)=∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​)+i∂x∂v​(x0​,y0​)=∂y∂v​(x0​,y0​)−i∂y∂u​(x0​,y0​).

And uuu and vvv have continuous partial derivatives at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​).

And let c=∂u∂x(x0,y0)c=\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)c=∂x∂u​(x0​,y0​) and d=∂v∂x(x0,y0)d=\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}(x_0,y_0)d=∂x∂v​(x0​,y0​).

Then f′(z0)=c+idf'(z_0)=c+idf′(z0​)=c+id, is holomorphic at z0z_0z0​.

Holomorphic Functions

Definition 2.8 (Holomorphic functions)

A function f:G→Cf:G\to \mathbb{C}f:G→C is holomorphic (or analytic) at z0∈Gz_0\in Gz0​∈G if it is complex differentiable at z0z_0z0​.

Note that the true definition of analytic function is that can be written as a convergent power series in a neighborhood of each point in its domain. We will prove that these two definitions are equivalent to each other in later sections.

Example:

Suppose f:G→Cf:G\to \mathbb{C}f:G→C where f=u+ivf=u+ivf=u+iv and ∂f∂x=∂u∂x+i∂v∂x\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}+i\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}∂x∂f​=∂x∂u​+i∂x∂v​, ∂f∂y=∂u∂y+i∂v∂y\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}=\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}+i\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}∂y∂f​=∂y∂u​+i∂y∂v​.

Define ∂∂z=12(∂∂x−i∂∂y)\frac{\partial}{\partial z}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}-i\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)∂z∂​=21​(∂x∂​−i∂y∂​) and ∂∂zˉ=12(∂∂x+i∂∂y)\frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{z}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}+i\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)∂zˉ∂​=21​(∂x∂​+i∂y∂​).

Suppose fff is holomorphic at zˉ0∈G\bar{z}_0\in Gzˉ0​∈G (Cauchy-Riemann equations hold at zˉ0\bar{z}_0zˉ0​).

Then ∂f∂zˉ(zˉ0)=0\frac{\partial f}{\partial \bar{z}}(\bar{z}_0)=0∂zˉ∂f​(zˉ0​)=0.

Note that ∀m∈Z\forall m\in \mathbb{Z}∀m∈Z, zmz^mzm is holomorphic on C\mathbb{C}C.

i.e. ∀a∈C\forall a\in \mathbb{C}∀a∈C, lim⁡z→azm−amz−a=(z−a)(zm−1+zm−2a+⋯+am−1)z−a=mam−1\lim_{z\to a}\frac{z^m-a^m}{z-a}=\frac{(z-a)(z^{m-1}+z^{m-2}a+\cdots+a^{m-1})}{z-a}=ma^{m-1}limz→a​z−azm−am​=z−a(z−a)(zm−1+zm−2a+⋯+am−1)​=mam−1.

So polynomials are holomorphic on C\mathbb{C}C.

So rational functions p/qp/qp/q are holomorphic on C∖{z∈C:q(z)=0}\mathbb{C}\setminus\{z\in \mathbb{C}:q(z)=0\}C∖{z∈C:q(z)=0}.

Definition 2.9 (Complex partial differential operators)

Let f:G→Cf:G\to \mathbb{C}f:G→C, f=u+ivf=u+ivf=u+iv, be a function defined on an open set G⊂CG\subset \mathbb{C}G⊂C.

Define:

∂∂xf=∂u∂x+i∂v∂x,∂∂yf=∂u∂y+i∂v∂y.\frac{\partial}{\partial x}f=\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}+i\frac{\partial v}{\partial x},\quad \frac{\partial}{\partial y}f=\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}+i\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}.∂x∂​f=∂x∂u​+i∂x∂v​,∂y∂​f=∂y∂u​+i∂y∂v​.

And

∂∂zf=12(∂∂x−i∂∂y)f,∂∂zˉf=12(∂∂x+i∂∂y)f.\frac{\partial}{\partial z}f=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}-i\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)f,\quad \frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{z}}f=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}+i\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)f.∂z∂​f=21​(∂x∂​−i∂y∂​)f,∂zˉ∂​f=21​(∂x∂​+i∂y∂​)f.

This definition of partial differential operators on complex functions is consistent with the definition of partial differential operators on real functions.

∂∂xf=∂∂zf+∂∂zˉf,∂∂yf=i(∂∂zf−∂∂zˉf).\frac{\partial}{\partial x}f=\frac{\partial}{\partial z}f+\frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{z}}f,\quad \frac{\partial}{\partial y}f=i\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial z}f-\frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{z}}f\right).∂x∂​f=∂z∂​f+∂zˉ∂​f,∂y∂​f=i(∂z∂​f−∂zˉ∂​f).

Curves in C\mathbb{C}C

Definition 2.11 (Curves in C\mathbb{C}C)

A curve γ\gammaγ in G⊂CG\subset \mathbb{C}G⊂C is a continuous map of an interval I∈RI\in \mathbb{R}I∈R into GGG. We say γ\gammaγ is differentiable if ∀t0∈I\forall t_0\in I∀t0​∈I, γ′(t0)=lim⁡t→t0γ(t)−γ(t0)t−t0\gamma'(t_0)=\lim_{t\to t_0}\frac{\gamma(t)-\gamma(t_0)}{t-t_0}γ′(t0​)=limt→t0​​t−t0​γ(t)−γ(t0​)​ exists.

If γ′(t0)\gamma'(t_0)γ′(t0​) is a point in C\mathbb{C}C, then γ′(t0)\gamma'(t_0)γ′(t0​) is called the tangent vector to γ\gammaγ at t0t_0t0​.

Definition of regular curves in C\mathbb{C}C

A curve γ\gammaγ is regular if γ′(t)≠0\gamma'(t)\neq 0γ′(t)=0 for all t∈It\in It∈I.

Definition of angle between two curves

Let γ1,γ2\gamma_1,\gamma_2γ1​,γ2​ be two curves in G⊂CG\subset \mathbb{C}G⊂C with γ1(t0)=γ2(t0)=z0\gamma_1(t_0)=\gamma_2(t_0)=z_0γ1​(t0​)=γ2​(t0​)=z0​ for some t0∈I1∩I2t_0\in I_1\cap I_2t0​∈I1​∩I2​.

The angle between γ1\gamma_1γ1​ and γ2\gamma_2γ2​ at z0z_0z0​ is the angle between the vectors γ1′(t0)\gamma_1'(t_0)γ1′​(t0​) and γ2′(t0)\gamma_2'(t_0)γ2′​(t0​). Denote as arg⁡(γ2′(t0))−arg⁡(γ1′(t0))=arg⁡(γ2′(t0)γ1′(t0))\arg(\gamma_2'(t_0))-\arg(\gamma_1'(t_0))=\arg(\gamma_2'(t_0)\gamma_1'(t_0))arg(γ2′​(t0​))−arg(γ1′​(t0​))=arg(γ2′​(t0​)γ1′​(t0​)).

Theorem 2.12 of conformality

Suppose f:G→Cf:G\to \mathbb{C}f:G→C is holomorphic function on open set G⊂CG\subset \mathbb{C}G⊂C and γ1,γ2\gamma_1,\gamma_2γ1​,γ2​ are regular curves in GGG with γ1(t0)=γ2(t0)=z0\gamma_1(t_0)=\gamma_2(t_0)=z_0γ1​(t0​)=γ2​(t0​)=z0​ for some t0∈I1∩I2t_0\in I_1\cap I_2t0​∈I1​∩I2​.

If f′(z0)≠0f'(z_0)\neq 0f′(z0​)=0, then the angle between γ1\gamma_1γ1​ and γ2\gamma_2γ2​ at z0z_0z0​ is the same as the angle between the vectors f′(z0)γ1′(t0)f'(z_0)\gamma_1'(t_0)f′(z0​)γ1′​(t0​) and f′(z0)γ2′(t0)f'(z_0)\gamma_2'(t_0)f′(z0​)γ2′​(t0​).

Lemma of function of a curve and angle

If f:G→Cf:G\to \mathbb{C}f:G→C is holomorphic function on open set G⊂CG\subset \mathbb{C}G⊂C and γ\gammaγ is differentiable curve in GGG with γ(t0)=z0\gamma(t_0)=z_0γ(t0​)=z0​ for some t0∈It_0\in It0​∈I.

Then,

(f∘γ)′(t0)=f′(γ(t0))γ′(t0).(f\circ \gamma)'(t_0)=f'(\gamma(t_0))\gamma'(t_0).(f∘γ)′(t0​)=f′(γ(t0​))γ′(t0​).

If Lemma of function of a curve and angle holds, then the angle between f∘γ1f\circ \gamma_1f∘γ1​ and f∘γ2f\circ \gamma_2f∘γ2​ at z0z_0z0​ is

arg⁡[(f∘γ2)′(t2)(f∘γ1)′(t1)]=⋯\begin{aligned} \arg\left[(f\circ \gamma_2)'(t_2)(f\circ \gamma_1)'(t_1)\right]&=\cdots \end{aligned}arg[(f∘γ2​)′(t2​)(f∘γ1​)′(t1​)]​=⋯​

Continue on Thursday. (Applying the chain rules)
